chemistry.scope_summary: Photochemical reduction of iron(III)-phenanthroline complex to iron(II)-phenanthroline complex using UV/visible light irradiation in flow injection system for simultaneous determination of iron(II) and iron(III)

optimization_table.key_findings: pH of about 4.7 adopted for determination; reaction tube length 200 cm for more than 95% photoreduction; tartrate concentration 5.0×10⁻⁴ mol dm⁻³

figures.flow_path: carrier solution flows through peristaltic pump, sample solutions are injected at two points (S1 and S2) via damper coil, flow passes through photochemical reactor, then reaction coil, and finally to spectrophotometric detector with flow cell
figures.reactor_geometry: flow-through photochemical reactor with quartz reaction tube
figures.light_source_position: external light sources (GGY-125 W fluorescent high-pressure mercury lamp and GGZ-125 W Vitalight lamp) positioned around quartz reaction tube
figures.key_information: FI system with photochemical reactor for iron(II) and iron(III) determination, uses dual injection points for simultaneous sample introduction
figures.caption: Fig.  1  Schematic diagram of the  FI  system.  P,  Peristaltic pump;  D,  damper coil (500 cm length;  1.0  mm i.d.);  S1  and  S2,  sample solutions  (100 mm3);  PR,  photochemical reactor;  R,  reaction coil for iron(i1)  and phen  (80  cm);  SP  spectrophotometric detector with flow cell (18  mm3 volume);  W,  waste; and  C,  carrier solution  | Fig.  2  Schematic diagram of the flow-through photochemical  reactor.  A,  Light source, one is a GGY-125  W  fluorescent high-  pressure mercury lamp and the other is a GGZ-125  W  Vitalight lamp;  B,  quartz reaction tube  (200  cm  x  0.8  mm i.d.)  | Fig.  3  Recorder signals for mixed standard solutions  of  iron(rri) and  iron(ii) with the double-injection  FI  system. Values above the peaks  indicate the concentration in ppm. The first peak corresponds to  iron(ii) and the second peak corresponds to total iron  | Fig.  4  Influence of pH. The percentage reduction values were  calculated with respect to the absorbance of 5.0 ppm of iron(iii)/  absorbance  of  5.0 ppm of iron(i1)

Fig.  5  The reduction  (%)  of  iron(111) was calculated as for Fig.  4  Influence  of  the length  of  the photochemical reaction tube.  | Fig.  6  Influence  of  tartrate, citrate and salicylate.  1,  0.1  mol dm-3  sodium acetate;  2, 0.1  rnol dm-3 sodium acetate  +  0.01  rnol dm-3  salicylic acid;  3, 0.1  rnol dm-3 sodium acetate  +  0.01  rnol dm-3  sodium citrate; and  4,O.l  mol dm-3 sodium acetate  +  0.01  rnol  dm-3  potassium sodium tartrate  | Fig.  7  Influence  of  tartrate concentration

translation_logic.batch_limitation: This is an analytical chemistry application, not a batch-to-flow translation
translation_logic.flow_advantage: Continuous automated analysis with dual injection capability for simultaneous determination of Fe(II) and total iron
translation_logic.what_changed: Implementation of flow injection system with photochemical reactor for automated analysis
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: Photochemical reduction mechanism and iron-phenanthroline complex chemistry

figure_descriptions: FI system schematic with photochemical reactor
Optimization plot showing tube length vs reduction efficiency

notes: This paper describes an analytical method rather than a synthetic batch-to-flow translation. The 98% yield refers to photoreduction efficiency at optimal conditions (200 cm tube length). No traditional batch baseline exists as this is purely an analytical flow injection application.
